tweets.text:
US tech fund flows are seeing extreme volatility:

US technology sector funds posted -$15 billion in outflows this week, the largest weekly withdrawal in at least 2.5 years.

This exceeds the previous largest weekly outflow over this period by +100%.

This also comes after +$19 billion in inflows the prior week, also the largest weekly inflow in at least 2.5 years.

Furthermore, US equity funds saw an -$8.5 billion outflow this week, the largest weekly withdrawal in 3 months, following a record +$119 billion inflow.

Year-to-date, US equity funds have attracted +$332 billion in inflows, accounting for half of the +$661 billion total across all developed markets.

Investors are cashing out massive profits.

tweets.text:
We are experiencing an unprecedented ETF boom:

US-listed ETFs have attracted over +$1 trillion in inflows so far in 2026, on track to surpass a record +$2 trillion by year-end.

That would mark the 4th consecutive annual increase in ETF inflows and exceed the 2025 record by +33%, or +$500 billion.

Meanwhile, 700 ETFs have launched year-to-date, on track for another annual record.

In June alone, 186 ETFs have debuted, the highest monthly total in history.

The ETF industry is expanding at a record pace.

tweets.text:
Retail investors appear to be rotating out of gold and Bitcoin into semiconductor stocks:

Since April, US gold and Bitcoin ETFs have posted -$12 billion in cumulative outflows.

Over the same period, US semiconductor ETFs have attracted +$20 billion in cumulative inflows.

This trend accelerated in mid-May, with outflows from gold and Bitcoin funds more than tripling.

At the same time, inflows into semiconductor ETFs have doubled.

Meanwhile, the largest US gold-backed ETF, $GLD, is down -13% since the start of April, while the largest Bitcoin ETF, $IBIT, is down -12%.

Over the same period, the semiconductor ETFs, $SOXX and $SMH, are up +81% and +60%, respectively.

Retail is driving markets like never before.

tweets.text:
The market's rally is broadening:

The S&P 500 equal-weight ETF, $RSP, has outperformed the S&P 500 ETF, $SPY, by +5.0 percentage points so far in June, on track for its best monthly outperformance since 2009.

That would also mark $RSP's 2nd-best month relative to $SPY in at least 23 years.

This comes as $RSP is up +1.9% month-to-date while $SPY is down -3.1% over the same period.

This week alone, the equal-weight ETF, $RSP, outperformed the S&P 500 ETF, $SPY, by +3.4 percentage points, the largest weekly outperformance since the 2020 pandemic.

As a result, the 52-week correlation between the S&P 500 and the S&P 500 equal-weighted index is down to 0.90, from 0.97 in 2022, the lowest since 2003.

Mega-cap tech leadership is taking a breather.

thread_tweets.text: There's so much to takeaway from this movie but one thing that i thought was so interesting was the Poet school ppl complain that only privilaged ppl go into arts these days but then when they find a lower income young poet who writes amazing stuff about her room, the tree outside etc they try and get her to write about socioeconomic sht bc the rich 'supporters' will like that more. Anyway, the guy who wrote this movie managed to use a little world to say some big things- one of those films you could discuss for an hour after its over.
thread_tweets.full_text: There's so much to takeaway from this movie but one thing that i thought was so interesting was the Poet school ppl complain that only privilaged ppl go into arts these days but then when they find a lower income young poet who writes amazing stuff about her room, the tree outside etc they try and get her to write about socioeconomic sht bc the rich 'supporters' will like that more. Anyway, the guy who wrote this movie managed to use a little world to say some big things- one of those films you could discuss for an hour after its over.

tweets.full_text:
Watched “A Poet” during vaca, indie film from Colombia which got 100% on RT (54/54 positive critic reviews) yet no one in it has ever acted bf. Wild, right? Anyway, I loved it. It’s about a lovable but tortured soul poet grappling w his life, family & society. I give it 9/10. https://t.co/ikOQM4vAv8

There's so much to takeaway from this movie but one thing that i thought was so interesting was the Poet school ppl complain that only privilaged ppl go into arts these days but then when they find a lower income young poet who writes amazing stuff about her room, the tree outside etc they try and get her to write about socioeconomic sht bc the rich 'supporters' will like that more. Anyway, the guy who wrote this movie managed to use a little world to say some big things- one of those films you could discuss for an hour after its over.

Government handouts don't shrink the wealth gap. 

They create it.

Washington prints a dollar, hands it to you, and the moment you spend it, that dollar lands in an asset the top 10% already owns. 

It passes through your hands and ends up in their pocket 🧵 https://t.co/jUAIpcFICc

thread_tweets.text:
Steps 1 and 2: the government printed $7.4 trillion in new money and sent it to everyone, most didn't need it. 

Transfer payments hit $6.1 trillion.

Consumers spent every check on contact, retail sales spiking each round. 

Step 4 shows where that money actually went. https://t.co/5AUeQy9HEE
thread_tweets.full_text:
Steps 1 and 2: the government printed $7.4 trillion in new money and sent it to everyone, most didn't need it. 

Transfer payments hit $6.1 trillion.

Consumers spent every check on contact, retail sales spiking each round. 

Step 4 shows where that money actually went. https://t.co/5AUeQy9HEE

thread_tweets.text:
Step 5: so who owns the assets your dollar just inflated?

Not you. 

The top 1% owns 50% of the stock market. 

The top 10% owns 93%. 

The bottom 50% of the country owns 1%.

Hand money to people who spend it and you hand wealth to the people who own what they buy. https://t.co/7Pnu4E4q2G

thread_tweets.text:
Step 4: 

Housing rose 54% since 2020. 

The S&P 500 $SPX rose 133%. 

The cost of living rose 29%. 

The consumer already spent the check, so they have less. 

Then they get taxed by inflation on the rest while their wage lags behind the cost of living. https://t.co/r1Cb2cANqU

Housing rose 54% since 2020. 

The S&P 500 $SPX rose 133%. 

The cost of living rose 29%. 

The consumer already spent the check, so they have less. 

Then they get taxed by inflation on the rest while their wage lags behind the cost of living. https://t.co/r1Cb2cANqU

And by the way, US government debt increased by 70% over the same time period. 

"Debt doesn't matter" 

Then why do we pay taxes? 

The poor and middle class will foot the bill on that too. https://t.co/jjyP3co7Bn

Step 6: six years of "help!" totaled up.

The $SPX doubled, up 133%. 

Your paycheck grew 32% on paper. 

After inflation, your actual buying power grew maybe ~2%.

You earned a bigger number and could afford less. 

The owners of the assets you inflated kept the difference. https://t.co/2YaPaNPYYg

If you enjoyed this thread, consider joining 4,000 investors and advisors who get my weekly report 👇
https://t.co/ZkGXJSv6aS

It is impossible for the gov to print money w/o widening the wealth gap, b/c the money always ends up where the assets already sit. 

Universal income is the same concept as this and would result in the same conclusion. More debt, more inflation, rich get richer. Poor get poorer

tweets.text: On this day in 1967, Barclays Bank unveiled the world's first cash machine outside its Enfield branch in north London. Inventor John Shepherd-Barron came up with the idea after arriving one minute too late to his bank on a Saturday and finding the doors locked. He originally planned a six-digit security code, but his wife said she could only remember four digits, and the four-digit PIN became the global standard. The device accepted special vouchers treated with mildly radioactive carbon-14 and dispensed up to £10 at a time. ATMs became one of banking's most profitable channel innovations: by the mid-2000s, the global network exceeded 3 million machines and generated billions in interchange and surcharge fees while letting banks cut branch staff costs by roughly 30%.

The device accepted special vouchers treated with mildly radioactive carbon-14 and dispensed up to £10 at a time. tweets.text:
10 years ago: 30-yr mortgage rate was 3.5% & average new home price in the US was $350k

Today: 30-yr mortgage rate is 6.5% & average new home price is $540k

Result: $38k increase in down payment (assuming 20% down) and 117% increase in monthly payment (from $1,257 to $2,731)

This is a fair tax policy?

tweets.text:
Good Morning from Germany, where the power market just delivered a lesson in renewable volatility.
Despite sunshine and record solar output, electricity prices surged >€700/MWh this week. Tuesday’s avg was still >€200/MWh.
Why? Wind generation collapsed. Plenty of sun. Almost no wind. Costly electricity.
